name: Tsukuyomi
description: Tsukuyomi, also known as Tsukiyomi-no-Mikoto, is the moon god in Japanese mythology and the Shinto religion. Born from Izanagi, the creator god, as he washed his right eye after escaping the underworld, Yomi, Tsukuyomi is considered one of the three noble children, alongside Amaterasu (the sun goddess) and Susanoo (the storm god). While his gender is sometimes debated, he is generally depicted as male. Tsukuyomi is associated with the moon, night, and time. His role in the myths is less prominent than Amaterasu and Susanoo, and his personality is often perceived as aloof and enigmatic. A key myth involving Tsukuyomi describes his anger and subsequent slaying of the food goddess Uke Mochi after she produced food from her nose, mouth, and anus. Amaterasu, disgusted by this act, refused to ever face Tsukuyomi again, leading to the separation of day and night. Though less detailed than other deities, Tsukuyomi remains an important figure representing the lunar cycle and the passage of time, a constant presence illuminating the night sky. His influence is felt in various Shinto rituals and beliefs, connecting him to cycles of growth and decay.
subtitle: God of the Moon and Night

keyMyths.title: Birth of Tsukuyomi
keyMyths.description: Tsukuyomi was born from Izanagi's right eye after he purified himself from his journey to the underworld. This made him one of the three noble children.

keyMyths.title: The Slaying of Uke Mochi
keyMyths.description: Tsukuyomi killed the food goddess Uke Mochi after being disgusted by her method of producing food. This act angered Amaterasu.

keyMyths.title: Separation of Day and Night
keyMyths.description: After Tsukuyomi slew Uke Mochi, Amaterasu refused to ever face him again, resulting in the separation of day and night. They inhabit separate realms.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings of Rice and Sake
worshipPractices.description: In Shinto shrines dedicated to Tsukuyomi, offerings of rice, sake, and other agricultural products are common. These offerings symbolize gratitude for the moon's influence on harvests and fertility.

worshipPractices.name: Ritualistic Dances
worshipPractices.description: Some Shinto rituals involve dances performed under the moonlight to honor Tsukuyomi. These dances often mimic the moon's phases and are meant to invoke his blessings.

festivals.name: Tsukimi (Moon Viewing Festival)
festivals.description: Tsukimi is a traditional Japanese festival celebrating the beauty of the harvest moon. People gather to view the moon, offer seasonal fruits and vegetables, and enjoy moon-viewing parties.

festivals.name: Mid-Autumn Festival
festivals.description: Celebrated in some parts of Japan with Chinese cultural influence, this festival involves mooncakes and lanterns, similar to those found in Chinese Mid-Autumn Festival traditions, in honor of the moon.

cultural.modernLegacy: Tsukuyomi continues to inspire modern art, literature, and popular culture. He frequently appears in anime, manga, and video games, often portrayed as a mysterious and powerful figure associated with the moon and night. His name and symbolism are also used in branding and design.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Tsukuyomi's birth is a pivotal moment in Japanese cosmogony. According to the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, the two primary chronicles of Japanese mythology, Tsukuyomi was born when the god Izanagi washed his right eye after returning from Yomi, the land of the dead. This act of purification gave rise to Tsukuyomi, who embodies the essence of the moon. He is considered one of the 'three noble children' (三貴子, Mihashira no Uzunomiko), along with Amaterasu, the sun goddess, born from Izanagi's left eye, and Susanoo, the storm god, born from his nose. These three deities are central to the Shinto pantheon, each ruling over different aspects of the cosmos. The circumstances of Tsukuyomi's birth link him to purity and the liminal space between life and death, reflecting the moon's association with cycles and transitions. While the myths clearly state his origin, Tsukuyomi's role, in comparison to his siblings, is less defined in early narratives, contributing to a certain mystique surrounding him.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: While Tsukuyomi's stories are fewer compared to Amaterasu and Susanoo, one significant myth involves his visit to Ukemochi-no-Kami, the goddess of food. In some versions, Ukemochi produced food from her body to feed Tsukuyomi. Disgusted by this method, Tsukuyomi slew Ukemochi. When Amaterasu heard of this, she became angry and refused to ever face Tsukuyomi again, leading to the separation of the sun and moon, and thus day and night. This story highlights themes of purity, disgust, and the consequences of actions. Other accounts portray Tsukuyomi as a more benevolent figure, focusing on his role in regulating time and cycles. The scarcity of detailed myths surrounding Tsukuyomi has led to various interpretations of his character and role in the Shinto pantheon. He is often seen as a more passive observer compared to his siblings, embodying the quiet, reflective nature of the moon.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Worship of Tsukuyomi is less prominent than that of Amaterasu or Susanoo, but dedicated shrines and rituals exist. Some shrines, such as the Tsukuyomi Shrine in Kyoto, are specifically dedicated to him. Offerings of rice, sake, and other agricultural products are common, reflecting the moon's influence on agriculture and fertility. Tsukuyomi is often invoked for blessings related to safe travel at night, protection from darkness, and the regulation of time. The absence of a widespread, formalized cult may be attributed to the moon's perceived passivity compared to the active forces of the sun and storms. However, Tsukuyomi's presence is subtly woven into various Shinto practices and beliefs, particularly those related to purification and cyclical renewal. His association with water, derived from his birth during Izanagi's ablutions, further connects him to purification rituals.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Tsukuyomi's legacy extends beyond religious worship and permeates Japanese culture. He serves as a potent symbol of the moon, night, and the passage of time. In art and literature, Tsukuyomi is often depicted as a serene and mysterious figure, embodying the quiet beauty of the lunar realm. Modern interpretations of Tsukuyomi appear in various forms of popular culture, including anime, manga, and video games. He is frequently portrayed as a powerful and enigmatic character, often associated with lunar magic or celestial powers. His name and imagery are also used in branding, design, and even scientific endeavors, reflecting the enduring fascination with the moon and its influence on human life. Tsukuyomi's continued presence in contemporary society underscores the lasting impact of Japanese mythology and the enduring power of celestial symbolism.

shortDescription: Tsukuyomi is the Japanese moon god, born from Izanagi. He is one of the three noble children and plays a significant role in Japanese mythology.
